1:
“After these things Jesus shewed himself again to the disciples at the sea of Tiberias; and on this wise shewed he himself.” The witnesses note that this chapter, following what seemed the close of the Gospel (John 20:30–31), adds a further resurrection appearance. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the place as the Sea of Galilee (Matthew 4:18), where Jesus had promised to meet his disciples (Mark 14:28; Matthew 28:10). Robertson notes the verb “manifested himself” (ephanerōsen heauton), and that the risen Jesus was “only seen during the forty days now and then” (Acts 1:3), ten such instances being recorded. Augustine, for the Latin fathers, observes that “the preceding words seem to indicate the end of the book,” yet the Evangelist “goes on farther” to relate this appearance by the sea.

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the purpose: the Evangelist “still labours to prove the resurrection of Christ,” relating that he “appeared to seven disciples,” whose testimony, given by men once slow to believe, is the more to be trusted. The witnesses thus find in the verse the opening of a further proof and a further lesson. The proof is the reality of the risen Christ, who shows himself yet again, bodily and unmistakably, to a company of his own; the resurrection rests not on a single sight but on repeated, varied appearances over forty days. The lesson, the witnesses note, lies in the manner — “on this wise shewed he himself” — for what follows is no mere apparition but a scene of teaching and restoration, in which the risen Lord meets his disciples at their old work, supplies their need, and recommissions the one who had denied him. The chapter that seems an appendix is, the witnesses observe, a precious record of the living Christ’s continuing care for his church.

The witnesses observe that this added chapter crowns the Gospel with a scene of grace. Having proved the resurrection by the appearances in the upper room, John records one more by the lake, where doctrine gives way to tender dealing — the risen Lord feeding his disciples and restoring his fallen apostle. The verse thus opens not a mere supplement but a fitting close, showing that the same Christ who rose in power stoops in love to provide for and recommission his own.
2:
“There were together Simon Peter, and Thomas called Didymus, and Nathanael of Cana in Galilee, and the sons of Zebedee, and two other of his disciples.” The witnesses note the gathered company of seven. The grammatical-historical commentators count seven disciples, of whom five are named: Peter, Thomas, Nathanael, and the two sons of Zebedee — who, Robertson observes, are James and John, “mentioned by name nowhere in John’s Gospel, apparently because John is the author.” The two unnamed, Lightfoot conjectures, were “probably Philip and Andrew.” Gill notes they were “together… in one house” in Galilee, not far from the sea and the mountain where Christ had appointed to meet them (Matthew 28:16). Barnes observes that, “while they were waiting for the promise of the Holy Spirit,” they “still found it proper to be usefully employed.”

The witnesses dwell on the little fellowship and its season. These were the days between the resurrection and Pentecost, the disciples gathered in Galilee, waiting upon the Lord’s further word and the promised Spirit. The witnesses note the fitness of the company: Peter, who had denied his Master and was soon to be restored; Thomas, who had doubted and now believed; Nathanael, the guileless Israelite (John 1:47); and John, the beloved disciple, who veils his own name and his brother’s under “the sons of Zebedee.” They are found together, in the bond of common discipleship, awaiting their Lord. The witnesses find here a quiet picture of the waiting church — not yet endued with power from on high, uncertain what their future service would be, yet keeping together and ready to be employed. It is to such a company, gathered and waiting, that the risen Christ comes; and the naming of the seven, by an eyewitness who was himself among them, sets the scene with the exactness of one who was there.

The witnesses note that the company is named with an eyewitness’s precision, and that the mingling of the once-doubting and the once-denying with the faithful marks it as a true picture of the church. None of these men had earned the visit they were about to receive; they are simply found together, waiting and working, when the Lord drew near. The verse thus reminds the reader that Christ comes to his people not in their strength but in their waiting, and gathers the weak and the strong alike into one expectant fellowship.
3:
“Simon Peter saith unto them, I go a fishing. They say unto him, We also go with thee. They went forth, and entered into a ship immediately; and that night they caught nothing.” The witnesses note Peter’s return to his old trade and the empty nets. The grammatical-historical commentators read the proposal as natural and innocent: Peter “had been a fisherman by practice” (Robertson, noting the infinitive of purpose, halieuein), and in the interval before Pentecost returned to honest work. Ellicott insists it “does not express either an abandonment of their higher calling” or unbelief, but “the vivid representation by an ear-witness of what actually took place as they returned to their ordinary work.” Clarke suggests that “the scandal of the cross” had cut off the charitable support they had known (Luke 8:3), so they fell back on their nets. The fruitless night — “that night they caught nothing” — Barnes reads as ordered “in the providence of God, that the miracle which was wrought might appear more remarkable.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, judges that Peter’s fishing “ought not to be regarded as inconsistent with his office,” for though Christ had ordained him an Apostle, “he abstained from the exercise of the apostleship for a short time, till he should be clothed with new power.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both a homely realism and a providential design. The realism is the apostles at their nets — waiting men, filling the interval with familiar labour, neither presuming to begin their great work before the Spirit came nor sitting idle. The design is the empty net: their whole night’s toil takes nothing, that the morning’s miraculous catch might shine the brighter, and that they might learn afresh, before they were sent to be fishers of men, that without Christ they “can do nothing” (John 15:5). The witnesses note the gentle preparation: the Lord lets them fail all night at their own craft, that he may meet them at dawn and fill their nets at his word.

The witnesses draw the practical lesson that honest labour is no betrayal of the higher calling. While they waited for the Spirit, the apostles did not despise common work; yet their fruitless night taught them that even at their own trade they could do nothing without their Lord. So the verse joins diligence and dependence: the disciple is to be busy in his calling, yet to learn afresh that the increase is Christ’s to give, and that the emptiest night may be the eve of the richest morning.
4:
“But when the morning was now come, Jesus stood on the shore: but the disciples knew not that it was Jesus.” The witnesses note the dawn appearance and the failure to recognise him. The grammatical-historical commentators picture the scene: Robertson reads the genitive absolute “when day was now breaking” (prōias ēdē ginomenēs), the dawn coming on while it was still dim; Jesus “stood on the shore” (John 20:19), appearing suddenly, “but no one knew whence or how” (Ellicott). The disciples “knew not that it was Jesus” — partly, Clarke and Barnes suggest, because of the twilight and the distance, partly, Wesley adds, because “their eyes were holden” (cf. Luke 24:16), as with the two on the Emmaus road. Gill finds comfort in the timing: “Christ visits his right early, and is a present help in time of trouble.”

The witnesses dwell on the tender providence of the scene. The Lord comes to his toiling, weary disciples at the breaking of day, after their fruitless night — standing on the shore, watching them, before they know him. The witnesses note that this not-knowing is of a piece with the other resurrection appearances, where Christ is often unrecognised at first (Mary at the tomb, the two at Emmaus), the eyes of his own being held until he chooses to reveal himself. They find in it both a historical truth — the reality of a risen body, present yet not always at once known — and a spiritual lesson: that Christ is often near his people in their darkest, most fruitless hours, watching their toil, before they discern his presence. The disciples labour all night and catch nothing, not knowing that the Lord stands on the shore; and the witnesses bid the reader take heart, that the same Saviour stands near in every weary watch, ready to make himself known and to turn the empty night into a morning of abundance.

The witnesses find comfort in the picture of Christ standing unseen on the shore. He had watched their toil all night, as he sees his people in every dark and fruitless hour, though they know it not. The verse thus assures the weary believer that the Lord is never far in the time of failure, but waits, often unrecognised, to turn the empty net into an overflowing one. The morning that broke on the disheartened fishermen brought their risen Master with it.
5:
“Then Jesus saith unto them, Children, have ye any meat? They answered him, No.” The witnesses dwell on the tender address and the question. The grammatical-historical commentators note the word “Children” (paidia): Robertson calls it “a colloquial expression like ‘my boys,’” a diminutive of affection, used by Jesus here alone in addressing his disciples and adopted by the aged Apostle John in his Epistle (1 John 2:18). Clarke insists it means “little children, or beloved children,” a term “of familiarity and affectionate kindness,” and marvels how the margin made “Sirs” of it. The question, “have ye any meat?” — the word denoting what is eaten with bread, especially fish (Barnes) — is framed, Robertson notes, so as to expect the answer “No,” which they give.

The witnesses dwell on the gentleness and the purpose of the question. The risen Lord, still unrecognised, draws near with a word of affection — “Children” — and a homely inquiry after their success. The witnesses note that the very tenderness of the address might have revealed him, as Lightfoot suggests, had their eyes not been held; it was a word they had often heard from his lips. The question, asked of one who knows the answer, is not for information but for instruction: it draws from them the confession of their failure — “No” — so that the empty net is owned before it is filled. The witnesses find here the way of Christ with his people: he comes near in love, leads them to acknowledge their own emptiness, and so prepares them to receive from his hand what their own labour could not win. The “No” of the weary fishermen is the necessary prelude to the great draught; and the affectionate “Children” of the unknown stranger is the first token that the One who stands on the shore is no stranger at all, but their loving Lord.

The witnesses dwell on the kindness of a question that seeks no information but an admission. Christ would have his disciples own their emptiness before he fills it, that the gift might be plainly his. So the affectionate ‘Children’ and the searching ‘have ye any meat?’ together prepare the way for the miracle: love draws near, failure is confessed, and the stage is set for the Lord to supply what their labour could not. The verse teaches that the honest ‘No’ of the helpless is the door to the abundance of Christ.
6:
“And he said unto them, Cast the net on the right side of the ship, and ye shall find. They cast therefore, and now they were not able to draw it for the multitude of fishes.” The witnesses dwell on the miraculous draught at Christ’s word. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Jesus, “willing to make himself known by a miracle, since they knew him not” (Gill), directs them to “the right side,” where “Jesus knew the fish were” (Robertson). The disciples, “being at a loss what to do, readily obey him, though they did not know who he was” (Calvin). The result is overwhelming: Robertson reads the imperfect “were not able to draw it” (ouketi ischyon) as “picturing the disciples tugging at the net,” which the multitude of fishes had filled. The scene closely echoes the earlier call by the same sea (Luke 5:6).

Wesley, for the Wesleyan, finds it more than a wonder of power: “not only a demonstration of the power of our Lord, but a kind supply for them and their families… and an emblem of the great success they should afterward have” in gathering men. Cyril of Jerusalem and others see in the draught a figure of the church’s ingathering by the Spirit. The witnesses thus find in the verse both a proof and a parable. As proof, it reveals the risen Christ, whose word commands the fish of the sea and fills in a moment the net that toiled all night in vain. As parable, it foreshadows the disciples’ coming work as “fishers of men”: cast where he bids, at his word, their labour — fruitless in their own strength — would gather a great multitude into the net of the gospel. The witnesses note the lesson woven through it: the same toil that took nothing all night takes more than it can hold the moment it is done at Christ’s command; and so the church learns that all fruitful service flows from obedience to the word of her risen Lord.

The witnesses press the abiding principle the miracle enshrines. The net cast in their own wisdom took nothing; the same net, cast at his word, could not be drawn for the multitude. So the verse stands as a parable of all gospel labour: fruitfulness is not the reward of effort but the gift of obedience, and the servant who works at Christ’s command and in Christ’s strength will gather where his own striving failed. The toiling church is bidden to listen for the word from the shore.
7:
“Therefore that disciple whom Jesus loved saith unto Peter, It is the Lord. Now when Simon Peter heard that it was the Lord, he girt his fisher’s coat unto him, (for he was naked,) and did cast himself into the sea.” The witnesses delight in the characteristic action of the two great apostles. The grammatical-historical commentators identify “that disciple whom Jesus loved” as John (John 13:23), and find in the scene “the traits of character which have before met us… exactly preserved” (Ellicott): John, “true to the life of contemplation, is first to trace” the Lord in the miracle and to say, “It is the Lord”; Peter, “true to the life of action,” is first to plunge toward him. Barnes notes John “was convinced by the apparent miracle”; Clarke explains the “fisher’s coat” (ependytēn, an upper garment) and that “naked” means only that Peter had been stripped of his outer coat for work (cf. 1 Samuel 19:24).

The witnesses dwell on the beautiful interplay of love’s two ways. John, the contemplative, perceives; Peter, the ardent, acts. The one knows the Lord by the eye of quiet discernment; the other, the moment he hears, cannot wait for the ship but girds himself and casts himself into the sea to reach his Master. The witnesses note the tenderness in Peter’s haste: this is the man who had denied his Lord, yet, far from shrinking, he is the first to rush toward him — love outrunning shame, eager to be near the One he had wronged. They find here a lesson in the variety of grace: the same Spirit makes one disciple swift to discern and another swift to act, and the church needs both — the seeing of John and the going of Peter. And they note the providence in it, for Peter’s plunge toward the shore prepares for the searching, restoring conversation that the risen Lord is about to hold with him over the breakfast fire.

The witnesses find in the two apostles a lasting picture of the body of Christ, where differing graces serve one love. John’s quiet discernment and Peter’s headlong ardour are not rivals but partners; the church needs the eye that perceives the Lord and the heart that runs to him. And in Peter’s eager plunge, the witnesses read the surest sign of a love not quenched by his fall: the denier is the first to reach the shore, drawn by a grace that had already begun to heal him before ever the question ‘Lovest thou me?’ was asked.
8:
“And the other disciples came in a little ship (for they were not far from land, but as it were two hundred cubits), dragging the net with fishes.” The witnesses note the other six bringing in the catch while Peter swam ahead. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the rest, more soberly than Peter, “came in the boat” (Ellicott, Robertson noting the diminutive ploiarion), being “not far from land, but as it were two hundred cubits” — about a hundred yards (Ellicott). They came “dragging the net with fishes,” unable to haul it aboard for the multitude, towing it to the shore instead. Clarke describes the manner of such fishing, the net stretched and drawn in toward land.

The witnesses find in the verse a quiet contrast and a homely truth. While Peter, all ardour, casts himself into the sea, the others keep to their work and bring in the precious draught — both, the witnesses note, are forms of love, the impetuous and the steady. They observe the eyewitness’s exactness: the distance from shore, the little boat, the net too full to lift, all recorded by one who was there and remembered every detail. And they note the providence in the heavy net dragged to land: the great catch, given at Christ’s word, is not lost in the disciples’ haste but carefully brought to the feet of the Lord who gave it. The witnesses thus read the verse as a small picture of faithful service — the fruit of obedience, gathered with labour and brought home to Christ — and as another touch confirming the reality of the scene, the boat, the cubits, the dragging net all marking the sober memory of a true event by the Sea of Galilee.

The witnesses note that not every disciple need follow Peter into the sea to prove his love; the steady labour of bringing in the net is its own devotion. So the verse honours the quieter service of those who keep to their post and finish the work, no less than the ardour of the one who runs ahead. The kingdom is served by both, and the great draught is brought safe to Christ by the patient hands that drag the net to land.
9:
“As soon then as they were come to land, they saw a fire of coals there, and fish laid thereon, and bread.” The witnesses note the meal already prepared on the shore. The grammatical-historical commentators picture the scene with the vividness of the original presents (Ellicott, Robertson): coming to land, “they see a fire of coals” (anthrakian, the same word as in John 18:18, where Peter had warmed himself at his denial), with “fish laid thereon, and bread.” Clarke and Barnes note that the provision “appears to have been a new miracle,” or at least a fresh supply not of the disciples’ own catching, since they had taken nothing all night (v.5) — bread and fish ready before they brought their own.

The witnesses dwell on the tender care the fire and food reveal. The risen Lord, who needs no food himself, has prepared a breakfast for his weary, hungry disciples — the coals kindled, the fish broiling, the bread ready — before ever they reach the shore. The witnesses note the gentle providence that needed not their catch at all: he who had filled their net could as easily provide the meal, and does, so that they come not to labour but to be fed. Some among the fathers and commentators have heard in the fire of coals an echo of the fire at which Peter denied his Lord (John 18:18), the same kind of fire now kindled in grace where he would be restored. The witnesses thus find in the verse a picture of the Saviour’s lovingkindness: he meets his own at the end of their fruitless night not with rebuke but with a warm fire and a ready meal, providing for their bodies as he is about to provide for Peter’s soul.

The witnesses find in the prepared fire a parable of grace. Before the disciples can offer anything, the Lord has already provided; the meal is spread before their catch is brought. So the verse pictures the gospel order, in which Christ’s provision goes before our work, and the weary are met not with demand but with welcome. And in the coals where Peter would soon be questioned, many have seen the gentleness of a Saviour who chooses the very setting of the denial to be the setting of the restoration.
10:
“Jesus saith unto them, Bring of the fish which ye have now caught.” The witnesses note that, though Christ had already provided a meal, he bids them bring of their own catch. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the fish they brought “furnished part of the meal of which they are about to partake” (Ellicott), joined to the provision already on the coals. Robertson notes the construction, “the fish which ye have now caught” — fish taken “by Christ’s direction” (v.6).

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the lesson of the verse: “though the net was filled in a moment, without any great labour on their part… the taking of them is not ascribed by Christ to the disciples” as if by their own skill — “thus we call the bread which we daily eat OUR bread, and yet, by asking that it may be given to us, we acknowledge that it proceeds from the blessing of God.” The witnesses thus find in the simple command a gentle dignity given to the disciples’ labour. The Lord has prepared the meal himself, yet he will have them bring of the fish he enabled them to catch, joining their gift to his provision. The witnesses note the grace in it: Christ does not need their fish, having his own ready, but he honours their work by receiving it, letting them contribute to the feast he has spread. So the verse pictures the partnership of grace and service — the increase wholly his gift, yet the disciples permitted to bring it and lay it at his feet, that what he gave they might offer back to him.

The witnesses observe the courtesy of a Lord who lets his servants share in the feast they did not provide. He needs nothing they can bring, yet he bids them bring it, joining their little to his abundance. So the verse teaches that Christ delights to receive the offerings of his people, not because he lacks, but because he loves to honour their labour. What grace enabled them to catch, grace now invites them to lay before him, that giver and receiver might rejoice together.
11:
“Simon Peter went up, and drew the net to land full of great fishes, an hundred and fifty and three: and for all there were so many, yet was not the net broken.” The witnesses dwell on the great number and the unbroken net. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Peter, at Christ’s command (Ellicott reads “therefore”), “went up” into the boat and “drew” the net ashore — doing now, Robertson observes, what the whole company had failed to do before (v.6). The number, “an hundred and fifty and three,” is given because it marked “a very unusual draught” (Barnes), the great fish actually counted; and the wonder is that, “for all there were so many, yet was not the net broken.” Robertson notes the precise tally of these “large” fish.

The witnesses note the much speculation, ancient and modern, over the number 153 — the fathers and others finding in it various symbolic meanings, though the plain sense is simply the exact count of a remarkable catch, recorded by an eyewitness. More agree on the significance of the unbroken net: where the earlier draught had broken the nets (Luke 5:6), this net held, and many have read in it a figure of the gospel net that, however great the multitude it gathers, shall not break nor lose its catch. The witnesses thus find in the verse both the exactness of true testimony — the counted fish, the great size, the whole net — and a quiet emblem of the church’s ingathering. As the net brought every fish safe to land without breaking, so the gospel, cast at Christ’s word, would gather a great multitude of every kind and bring them safe to the shore of his kingdom, none lost, the net unbroken. Peter, soon to be made a fisher of men, draws to land the figure of his own coming work.

The witnesses add that the scene rewards Peter’s eagerness with a share in the work: he who first reached the shore now draws the net to land. The whole, in its exactness and its symbolism, has fed the church’s reflection for centuries. Yet the surest lesson is the plainest — that the net of the gospel, cast at the word of Christ, gathers a great and varied multitude and holds them fast, none slipping through, none lost, until all are brought safe to the feet of the Lord on the eternal shore.
12:
“Jesus saith unto them, Come and dine. And none of the disciples durst ask him, Who art thou? knowing that it was the Lord.” The witnesses note the gracious invitation and the awe-filled silence. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that “Come and dine” is properly “Come and break your fast” (aristēsate, Robertson), the early morning meal (v.4); Robertson pictures “a delightful breakfast of fresh broiled fish… with the hush of joyful surprise in the presence of the Lord.” The disciples “durst not ask, Who art thou? knowing that it was the Lord” — they were sure of his identity, yet held in a reverent silence, half-awe and half-wonder at the risen One in their midst.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, observes that Christ did eat with them (as Luke records, Luke 24:43), “not to satisfy the wants of nature, but to show the reality of His resurrection” — though “the bodies of the just, when they rise again, shall need no food.” The Eastern Chrysostom makes the same point. Calvin, for the Reformed, asks what held the disciples from questioning, and finds it “shame arising from reverence” — not doubt, for they knew him, but a holy awe. The witnesses thus find in the verse a scene of tender majesty. The risen Lord plays the host at the lakeside breakfast, calling his weary fishermen to be fed; and they, certain it is he yet awed by the mystery of his risen presence, dare not break the silence with a question. The witnesses note the blend of intimacy and reverence that marks all true communion with Christ: near enough to share his table, yet bowed in wonder before the One who has conquered death and now breaks bread with his own.

The witnesses dwell on the union of nearness and awe in this breakfast. The disciples sit at the Lord’s table, yet none dares speak a needless word; they are his friends, and yet they tremble before the mystery of his risen life. So the verse models the spirit of all true worship, which draws near with confidence and bows with reverence. To know that it is the Lord is at once to be at home in his presence and silenced by his glory; and the lakeside meal becomes a quiet sacrament of communion with the living Christ.
13:
“Jesus then cometh, and taketh bread, and giveth them, and fish likewise.” The witnesses note the risen Lord acting as host at the feast. The grammatical-historical commentators picture the scene with the vivid presents (Robertson): Jesus “taketh the bread, and giveth them,” serving his disciples “as host at this early breakfast, his last recorded meal with these seven faithful followers.” Gill describes him taking “his place as the master of the feast, and head of the family,” distributing the bread and fish. Clarke notes that he ate with them as well (Luke 24:43). Ellicott and others hear in the taking and giving of bread an echo of the Last Supper (cf. John 20:22; Luke 24:30).

The witnesses dwell on the gentle grace of the act. The same hands that had been pierced now take the bread and give it to his own; the Lord of glory serves breakfast to his fishermen. The witnesses note how the gesture would have stirred their memory — of the feeding of the multitudes, when he took bread and gave (John 6:11), and of the upper room, where he broke bread the night before he died, and of Emmaus, where he was known “in breaking of bread.” In this familiar act the risen Christ both confirms that it is truly he and shows that his love and his lowly service are unchanged by his rising. The witnesses thus find in the verse a precious picture of fellowship restored: the disciples, scattered by his death, are gathered again at his table, fed by his hand, in quiet communion with their living Lord. The One who conquered death stoops to give them bread and fish — the risen Saviour still the servant of his people, still the host who spreads the table and bids them eat.

The witnesses find the deepest comfort in the constancy the gesture reveals. Resurrection has not changed the heart of Christ: the hands that broke bread in the upper room break it again on the shore, and the Lord who washed his disciples’ feet now serves them breakfast. So the verse assures the believer that the risen and reigning Saviour is the same who stooped to feed and serve, and that his exaltation has not lifted him above the lowly love that ministers to his own. He is the host of his people still.
14:
“This is now the third time that Jesus shewed himself to his disciples, after that he was risen from the dead.” The witnesses note John’s reckoning of this as the third appearance. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the count: Robertson and Clarke specify that John means the third time Jesus “was manifested to the disciples” as a body — the first on the resurrection evening (John 20:19), the second eight days later with Thomas (John 20:26), and now this third at the sea. The Evangelist “passes over… the appearances to Mary Magdalene and others” (Ellicott), counting only those to the assembled company, for there were other appearances besides (to Mary, to the women, to the two at Emmaus, to Peter).

Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that “all that had been transacted in one day is included in one manifestation,” so that the “third time” refers to distinct occasions, given “at intervals, in order to confirm” the disciples in the certainty of the resurrection. Augustine, for the Latin fathers, marks that with “this third manifestation… the Gospel of the blessed Apostle John is brought to a close.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the Evangelist’s careful witness to the resurrection. He numbers the appearances to guard the truth: the risen Christ was no momentary vision but showed himself repeatedly, to the gathered disciples, over many days, eating with them, teaching them, proving by every means that he was truly alive. The witnesses note the apologetic weight of the count — that faith rests not on a single sight but on a series of attested appearances — and the fitness of closing the Gospel with this third, in which the living Lord feeds his own and restores his fallen apostle, the resurrection issuing not in mere wonder but in the renewal of love and the recommissioning of his servants.

The witnesses note how fitting it is that the Gospel of life should close, not with the empty tomb alone, but with the living Lord at table with his own. The third manifestation gathers up the whole purpose of the resurrection — that Christ might be known, believed, loved, and obeyed by a restored and recommissioned people. So the verse sets the seal on John’s witness: the One who died is alive, has shown himself again and again, and lives to feed his church and to send his servants out in his name.
15:
“So when they had dined, Jesus saith to Simon Peter, Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou me more than these? He saith unto him, Yea, Lord; thou knowest that I love thee. He saith unto him, Feed my lambs.” The witnesses take this as the beginning of Peter’s restoration. The grammatical-historical commentators note the formal address, “Simon, son of John,” recalling the name before his calling, and the searching “more than these.” On “these” there is debate: Barnes notes the ambiguity — it may mean “these things” (his boat and nets) or “these” other disciples. Clarke and Robertson take it of the others, a gentle reproach, for Peter “had boasted that he would stand by Christ though all men forsook him” (Mark 14:29). Clarke marks the design: Peter “had thrice denied his Lord, and now Christ gives him an opportunity… to repair his fault by a triple confession.”

The Eastern Chrysostom finds the lesson that the surest proof of love to Christ is “tender care for our neighbour,” which Christ at once requires — “Feed my lambs.” Theophylact notes that the superintendence of the flock is committed to Peter; Augustine, for the Latin fathers, that Christ asked knowing the answer, for Peter’s good. Calvin, for the Reformed, sees Peter “restored to that rank of honour from which he had fallen,” his denial having “rendered him unworthy of the apostleship.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the tender wisdom of the restoration. Christ does not upbraid Peter for his denial, but draws from him a confession of love — thrice, to answer the threefold denial — and to each confession joins a charge: “Feed my lambs.” The witnesses note that love to Christ and care for his flock are bound together: the proof of the one is the doing of the other, so that the restored apostle is set to shepherd the people his Lord died to save. Peter’s fall is not held against him; his love is owned, and his commission renewed.

The witnesses dwell on the gentleness of the method. Christ does not wring from Peter a grovelling apology, but lifts him up by asking after his love; and to the love confessed he at once entrusts the flock. So the verse teaches how the Lord deals with his fallen people — not casting them off, but probing the wound to heal it, and turning the restored sinner into a servant. The denier is not merely forgiven but employed; his very fall, met by grace, becomes the school in which he learns the tenderness with which to feed the lambs.
16:
“He saith to him again the second time, Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou me? He saith unto him, Yea, Lord; thou knowest that I love thee. He saith unto him, Feed my sheep.” The witnesses note the second question and the charge that follows. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the question is repeated “in exactly the same form, except that our Lord does not continue the comparison ‘more than these’” (Ellicott) — pressing now simply on Peter’s love, not his pre-eminence. The charge changes its word: “Feed my sheep,” where the verb (Robertson, poimaine, “shepherd, tend”) means more than feeding — “governing, caring for, guiding, protecting… the faithful vigilance which a shepherd uses to guide his flock” (Barnes). Where the first charge was “feed my lambs,” this is the broader pastoral oversight of the whole flock.

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks who the flock are: Christ “does not give to Peter and others the office of feeding all sorts of persons,” but his own sheep, who “hear my voice, and follow me” and “hear not the voice of a stranger” (John 10:27; John 10:5). The witnesses thus find in the second exchange the deepening of Peter’s commission. Again he is asked to own his love, and again, on his confession, he is charged with the care of Christ’s people — now under the fuller image of the shepherd who not only feeds but guides, guards, and rules the flock. The witnesses note the dignity and the burden laid on Peter and, through him, on every pastor: the sheep are Christ’s, not his own, entrusted to his keeping; and the love he professes to the Shepherd is to be proved by his shepherding of the Shepherd’s flock. The repeated question and charge teach that there is no love to Christ that does not issue in service to his people, and no pastoral office that does not rest on love to Christ.

The witnesses note that the rising scale of the charges — lambs, then sheep, feeding and shepherding — sets before Peter the whole care of the church. Nothing is held back from the restored apostle; he is trusted with the weakest lambs and the whole flock alike. And the ground of it all is love to Christ, asked before the charge is given, so that the pastoral office is forever bound to personal devotion. The verse thus makes love to the Shepherd the one indispensable qualification for the keeping of his sheep.
17:
“He saith unto him the third time, Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou me? Peter was grieved because he said unto him the third time, Lovest thou me? And he said unto him, Lord, thou knowest all things; thou knowest that I love thee. Jesus saith unto him, Feed my sheep.” The witnesses dwell on the third question, Peter’s grief, and the change of word. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the threefold question answers the threefold denial: Christ “tenderly admonished him of his fault… while he solemnly charged him to be faithful” (Barnes). Many mark a shift in the Greek: in the first two questions Jesus used the higher word for love (agapaō), and Peter answered with the warmer, more personal phileō; now, the third time, “Jesus picks up the word phileō used by Peter and challenges that” (Robertson) — as if to ask, “Dost thou indeed even love me with the love of a friend?” Peter “was grieved” (elypēthē) — Chrysostom suggesting he feared Christ saw some hidden weakness in him, as he had foreseen his denial — and casts himself wholly on Christ’s knowledge: “Lord, thou knowest all things; thou knowest that I love thee.”

The witnesses note the various readings of the agapaō/phileō interchange — some holding the distinction deliberate, others that the words are used interchangeably in John — but agree on the heart of the scene. Calvin, for the Reformed, observes that Peter “did not perceive the object Christ had in view in putting the same question so frequently,” and thought himself “indirectly accused.” The witnesses thus find in the third exchange the depth of the restoration. The question that grieved Peter was meant to heal him: each asking probed the wound of his denial, and each answer drew out afresh his love, until the man who had thrice denied had thrice confessed. His final appeal — “thou knowest all things” — is the humbled confidence of a restored sinner, who, no longer trusting his own boasts, rests wholly on the Lord’s own knowledge of his heart. And again the charge follows, “Feed my sheep”: the fully restored apostle is fully recommissioned to the care of the flock.
18:
“Verily, verily, I say unto thee, When thou wast young, thou girdedst thyself, and walkedst whither thou wouldest: but when thou shalt be old, thou shalt stretch forth thy hands, and another shall gird thee, and carry thee whither thou wouldest not.” The witnesses take this as Christ’s foretelling of Peter’s martyrdom. The grammatical-historical commentators read the contrast of youth and age (Robertson noting the imperfects of customary action): in his vigour Peter “did as he pleased — he girded himself” and went where he would (Barnes); but in age he would “stretch forth his hands” and be girded and carried by another “whither he would not.” Clarke and Wesley find in “stretch forth thy hands” and being “girded” and “carried” a plain description of crucifixion — the Roman custom of binding the condemned to the cross, the hands outstretched. The Eastern Chrysostom reads the whole as Christ informing Peter “of his future martyrdom… an intimation to us how we should love.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, finds Christ here arming Peter “to maintain the warfare which was approaching,” demanding “not only faithfulness and diligence, but invincible courage in the midst of dangers, and firmness in bearing the cross.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a solemn honour conferred on the restored apostle. The man who once, to save his life, denied his Lord is told that he will one day lay down his life for him — the threefold denier made, by grace, a martyr. The witnesses note the tenderness and the cost together: Christ does not hide from Peter the hard end of his discipleship, but foretells the cross that awaits him, “whither thou wouldest not,” for nature shrinks from death even where love will embrace it. The prophecy, the witnesses observe, both seals Peter’s restoration — he who denied will be faithful unto death — and sets before every disciple the truth that to follow Christ is to take up the cross, and that the love which feeds his sheep may be called at last to die for his name.

The witnesses observe the perfect reversal grace has wrought. The Peter who girded himself and went where he would, who in his self-confidence followed afar and denied, will in his age be girded by another and carried where nature would not go — and will glorify God in it. The verse thus shows that the love now restored will be proved to the uttermost; the disciple healed of his denial will not deny again, but will stretch out his hands upon a cross. What he could not do in his own strength he will do by the grace that made him faithful unto death.
19:
“This spake he, signifying by what death he should glorify God. And when he had spoken this, he saith unto him, Follow me.” The witnesses note the Evangelist’s comment and the renewed call. The grammatical-historical commentators recognise the parenthesis as “a comment by the writer, quite in St. John’s style” (Ellicott; cf. John 12:33; John 18:32): the foretold death was a martyr’s death, “such as to honour God” (Barnes). The ancient tradition, which Clarke and Robertson report, is that Peter was crucified at Rome about thirty-four years later, and “deemed it so glorious a thing to die for Christ that he begged to be crucified with his head downwards,” counting himself unworthy to die as his Lord did. Then comes the word “Follow me” — at once a literal bidding (Peter rises and follows along the shore) and a summons to follow Christ even to the cross.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, ponders why the Lord says “Follow me” to Peter and not to the others present, and finds in it Peter’s special call to follow Christ in martyrdom. Calvin, for the Reformed, dwells on the honour of glorifying God by death, by which the martyrs “seal the Gospel of Christ with their blood.” Wesley adds that “it is not only by acting, but chiefly by suffering, that the saints glorify God.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the crown of Peter’s restoration. His denial is so fully healed that he is called to the highest fellowship with his Lord — to follow him even to a cross, and so to glorify God in his death. The witnesses note the grace in the “Follow me”: the same word with which Christ first called the fisherman (Matthew 4:19) is spoken again to the restored apostle, as if to begin his discipleship anew — only now the path he is called to follow leads, like his Master’s, to a cross, where his death shall glorify the God his life had sometimes failed.

The witnesses find in ‘Follow me’ the whole of discipleship gathered into two words. To follow Christ is to go his way, even to his cross, and so to glorify God by a death like his. The verse thus joins the beginning and the end of Peter’s walk: called from his nets with this same word, he is recalled to it now, that he may follow his Lord through suffering to glory. And it speaks to every believer, for the path of all who love Christ is to follow him — in life by obedience, and, if he wills, in death by a faithful witness that honours God.
20:
“Then Peter, turning about, seeth the disciple whom Jesus loved following; which also leaned on his breast at supper, and said, Lord, which is he that betrayeth thee?” The witnesses note Peter’s glance back at John as they follow the Lord. The grammatical-historical commentators picture the scene: Peter, rising to follow Christ (v.19), “turning about” (epistrapheis, Robertson’s sudden turn), sees John “following” at a distance — the beloved disciple, identified again by his place at the supper (John 13:25), naturally drawn to keep near his Lord and his friend Peter (Ellicott). Henry notes Peter “in obedience to his Master’s orders, followed him, and turning about” saw John behind.

The witnesses dwell on the natural human touch of the moment and its preparation for what follows. John, “more than any other,” had been the companion of Peter (Ellicott); the two are often together in the Gospels and the Acts. As Peter is called to follow Christ to a martyr’s death, he turns and sees the beloved disciple following too, and his thought goes at once to his friend’s future. The witnesses note the Evangelist’s modest self-identification — naming himself only as “the disciple whom Jesus loved,” who had leaned on his breast — so that the reader knows who is meant without his ever claiming the honour. The verse thus sets the stage for Peter’s question about John (v.21) and the Lord’s answer about each disciple’s distinct path. The witnesses find in it a gentle reminder that the followers of Christ, bound in love to one another, are yet each given their own way to follow him — and that Peter, just charged to mind his own discipleship, must learn not to be over-curious about his brother’s.

The witnesses note the quiet artistry by which the Evangelist names himself only in love’s terms — the disciple Jesus loved, who leaned on his breast. He claims no rank but the Master’s affection. And they observe how naturally the scene unfolds: Peter, his own future just disclosed, turns to his lifelong friend, and his thought runs to John’s lot as well. The verse thus prepares, with the truthfulness of memory, for the lesson the Lord is about to teach — that each disciple has his own appointed path, and must follow Christ without comparing his portion to another’s.
21:
“Peter seeing him saith to Jesus, Lord, and what shall this man do?” The witnesses note Peter’s curiosity about John’s future. The grammatical-historical commentators read the abrupt question — Robertson renders the Greek literally, “But this one… what?” (houtos de ti?) — as prompted, most agree, by “loving interest in the future of his friend” (Ellicott). Having just heard his own end foretold (vv.18–19), Peter turns to the beloved disciple following behind and asks what shall become of him: Barnes takes the question to mean “What death shall he die?”, Peter perhaps wondering whether John, the favoured disciple, should “have a happier lot.”

The witnesses dwell on the mingled good and ill in Peter’s question. There is affection in it — the natural concern of one friend for another, the two having shared the dark days and talked together of their Master’s words. But there is also, the witnesses note, a touch of the curiosity that looks aside from its own path to inquire into another’s. Peter has just been told to “follow me,” and his first impulse is to ask about John. The witnesses find here a very human moment, true to Peter’s warm and impulsive character, and the occasion of the Lord’s gentle correction to come. They observe that the question, though springing partly from love, needed the reproof Christ would give: the disciple charged with his own great calling and his own cross must not be over-busy about his brother’s lot, but must keep his eye on his Master and his own way of following him.

The witnesses note that Peter’s question is the very thing the Lord must correct. Scarcely commissioned and called afresh to follow, he is already glancing at his brother’s portion. The verse thus catches the perennial temptation of the disciple — to measure his own lot against another’s, to wonder why one is called to suffer and another spared. And it sets the stage for a word that has steadied the church ever since: that each servant has his own appointed way, and the business of every follower is not comparison but obedience.
22:
“Jesus saith unto him, If I will that he tarry till I come, what is that to thee? follow thou me.” The witnesses take this as Christ’s gentle rebuke of curiosity and renewal of Peter’s own call. The grammatical-historical commentators note the conditional form — Robertson reads “if I will” (ean thelō, third class) and “while I am coming” (heōs erchomai) — and the “sharp rebuke to Peter’s curiosity” in “what is that to thee?” Christ does not say John will tarry, but only, “If I will it, what concern is that of yours?” On “till I come,” the commentators offer several senses: the destruction of Jerusalem (Wesley — “he did tarry till Christ came to destroy Jerusalem”), the day of judgment, or simply that John should not die a violent death like Peter. The emphatic close turns Peter back to his duty: “follow thou me” — mind thine own discipleship.

Calvin, for the Reformed, is careful that the former clause not be read as a flat assertion that John would not die — a mistake, he notes, that arose by a slip of transcribers — for Christ speaks hypothetically. The witnesses thus find in the verse a word for every disciple tempted to compare his lot with another’s. Christ asserts his sovereign right to dispose of each of his servants as he wills — one to a martyr’s cross, another to a long life and a quiet end — and bids Peter not to question that disposal but to attend to his own following. The witnesses note the emphatic “thou”: “follow thou me,” whatever I appoint for him. The lesson, they find, is that the path of discipleship is personal and varied: each is given his own way to glorify God, and the believer’s business is not to scrutinise his brother’s calling but to be faithful in his own. Curiosity about another’s portion is rebuked; obedience in one’s own is commanded.

The witnesses press the largeness of the principle. Christ claims for himself the sovereign right to appoint to each of his servants a different path — one a swift martyrdom, another a long and quiet labour — and forbids the disciple to dispute or even to inquire into that disposal. The verse thus comforts and rebukes at once: it comforts, that our lot is in his wise and loving hand; it rebukes the restless curiosity that would compare callings. ‘Follow thou me’ is the whole of duty, whatever he wills for another.
23:
“Then went this saying abroad among the brethren, that that disciple should not die: yet Jesus said not unto him, He shall not die; but, If I will that he tarry till I come, what is that to thee?” The witnesses note the rumour that sprang from misunderstanding Christ’s words, and John’s careful correction. The grammatical-historical commentators explain how the mistake arose: from Christ’s words, “easily misunderstood to mean that he should not die,” and confirmed when John “survived all the other apostles” and lived to great age (Barnes). Robertson stresses that John “is anxious to set this right,” explaining that Jesus “was rebuking Peter’s curiosity, not affirming that John would live on till the Master returned.” The Evangelist carefully repeats the exact words: Christ said not “He shall not die,” but only the conditional, “If I will that he tarry.”

Wesley notes the “brethren” as “the Christians,” an appellation Christ himself taught (John 20:17), and adds that John himself, at the time of writing, “seems not to have known clearly whether he should die or not.” Calvin, for the Reformed, observes that the error arose among the disciples from misunderstanding, and that John writes to correct it. The witnesses thus find in the verse both a caution against misreading Christ’s words and a model of careful truthfulness. A saying of the Lord, taken beyond its meaning, had passed into a settled rumour; and the aged apostle, writing his Gospel, is at pains to lay the exact words before the church and to distinguish what Christ said from what men supposed he said. The witnesses note the lesson for every reader: how readily the words of Christ are stretched to fit our expectations, and how needful it is to hold to what he actually said. John will not let even a flattering legend about himself stand uncorrected, but sets the record straight — Jesus said not that he should not die.

The witnesses find in John’s careful correction a model of fidelity to the word of Christ. He will not let a pleasing rumour, even one that flattered him, pass as the Lord’s teaching, but lays the exact words before the church. The verse thus warns the reader how easily the sayings of Christ are bent to fit our hopes, and how needful it is to hold to what he actually said. Truth is guarded not by repeating the popular sense but by returning to the Lord’s own words.
24:
“This is the disciple which testifieth of these things, and wrote these things: and we know that his testimony is true.” The witnesses take this as the formal attestation of the Gospel’s authorship and truth. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the writer: “this is the disciple” just named (v.20), “the disciple whom Jesus loved” — Robertson finds here “a definite statement that the Beloved Disciple wrote this book.” Calvin notes that John, “having hitherto mentioned himself in the third person, now declares that it is himself, that greater weight may be attached to the statements of one who was an eyewitness.” The plural “we know that his testimony is true” raises the question of who speaks: Lightfoot and others discuss the change of person from John 19:35 (“he knoweth”) to “we know” here.

The witnesses divide on the “we.” Some, with Wesley, take it as the church’s endorsement appended to John’s Gospel — “the Church added these words, as Tertius did to Paul’s Epistle” (cf. Romans 16:22) — the testimony of those who knew John and vouched for him. Others, with Robertson and Calvin, take it as John’s own editorial plural, the apostle joining his readers’ assent to his witness, or the company of apostolic witnesses (Chrysostom: “we are witnesses of these things”). The witnesses thus find in the verse the Gospel’s solemn seal. Whether the church speaks here, or the apostle in the church’s name, the effect is the same: the book is attested as the true witness of the beloved disciple, who saw what he records and wrote it under the Spirit’s inspiration. The witnesses note the weight this lends to all that has gone before — not a tale at second hand, but the testimony of an eyewitness, known and trusted, whose record the church receives as true.

The witnesses dwell on the comfort this attestation gives to faith. The Gospel is no anonymous tale gathered from hearsay, but the witness of one who leaned on the Master’s breast, saw the cross and the empty tomb, and wrote what he knew. Whether the church here adds its endorsement or the apostle joins his readers’ assent, the verse sets the seal of trustworthy testimony on all that precedes. The believer rests his faith not on legend but on the record of a true and loving eyewitness.
25:
“And there are also many other things which Jesus did, the which, if they should be written every one, I suppose that even the world itself could not contain the books that should be written. Amen.” The witnesses take this closing verse as a reverent hyperbole on the inexhaustible fulness of Christ’s works. The grammatical-historical commentators note that John refers not to all he has recorded but to “the signs and wonders… which are neither recorded by him nor the other evangelists” (Gill) — the “many other things” beyond the chosen few (cf. John 20:30). The closing words are, Barnes says, “evidently the figure of speech called a hyperbole… where the words express more than is literally true,” common to all writers; Wesley notes that the softening “I suppose” (oimai, Robertson marking the first-person singular of the author) “shows that St. John wrote this verse.” The textual evidence, Ellicott observes, strongly supports the verse, though it is absent from the Sinaitic Codex.

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it as guarding the narrative from the charge of partiality: John “has passed over more than he has written,” recording only what relates to Christ’s “public office.” Henry, for the Puritan expositors, draws the devout response: “let us bless God for all that is in the Scriptures, and be thankful that there is so much in so small a space… Enough is recorded to direct our faith and regulate our practice; more would have been unnecessary.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a fitting close to the Gospel — a confession that the written record, full as it is, is but a fragment of the infinite riches of Christ. The deeds of the Son of God are beyond the compass of all books; the Evangelist has chosen out of a boundless store only what suffices to bring men to faith and life (John 20:31). The witnesses note the lesson of contentment and wonder: contentment, that what is written is enough for salvation; wonder, that the Christ who is so fully revealed is yet so far beyond all that can be told. The Gospel ends, fittingly, by pointing past itself to the immeasurable glory of its subject.
